meta.setting: Brush slopes, oak edges, rocky ledges, deer trails, and livestock country around French Gulch
meta.rdr_clone_target: RDR-clone western with tracking, livestock pressure, hunting restraint, fear, and hard choices

research_grounding.title: California Department of Fish and Wildlife Mountain Lion
research_grounding.use: Mountain lion habitat, solitary ambush behavior, prey signs, livestock conflict, drag marks, and covered carcasses.

research_grounding.title: California Department of Fish and Wildlife Deer
research_grounding.use: Deer presence across California as prey and tracking context.

generation_prompt.task: Generate the Cougar Trails cast page for the 1899 Jefferson State western packet. This JSON contains shape and guidance only.
page_shape: square image reference immediately after the title
opening blockquote with invented track note, horse warning, or brush saying generated later
H2 archetype subtitle in the register of Lion Shadow Country
one or two compact lore paragraphs introducing mountain lion sign as ambush pressure, livestock trouble, and proof the trail has watchers
H3 Role with a short table-facing function phrase
H3 Traits with two to four grounded signs or behaviors
10 H4 headings, each followed later by a brief playable move, warning, cost, edge, or complication
H3 Camp Say closing blockquote with plain meaning generated later
generation_guidance: Generate mountain lions as solitary ambush predators tied to brush, deer, rocky cover, and livestock conflict.
Tie future entries to drag marks, cached kills, plucked fur, nervous horses, nighttime screams, depredation claims, covered carcasses, and choosing restraint before panic.
Do not overuse attacks; absence, sign, and fear should matter more than spectacle.
Make the page useful for tracking, ranch disputes, animal safety, camp watch, and hard choices around predator killing.
Do not include final move names or final move text in this JSON.
generation_prompt.hand_cut_note: The page should feel like seeing nothing twice and then finding where the deer stopped. A cougar scene works best when the players realize the trail was being read back at them.
generation_prompt.table_spark: A cougar-trails scene should ask what left the sign, what the horses know, and whether the riders can act before fear picks the answer.
must_preserve: Use 1899 western animal-sign and trail language only.
Keep mountain lions naturalistic, solitary, and ecologically grounded.
The generated page must include image path cast/images/cougar-trails.jpg.
No finished page prose, final entries, or final sayings belong in this prompt file.
avoid: do not mention any prior packet, inherited taxonomy, or non-western source language
do not include medieval, enchanted, sacred-invented, or fake-tribal framing
do not make the cougar a monster, pet, boss fight, mascot, or moral symbol
do not use modern wildlife gear, vehicles, plastic props, or clean theme-park staging
self_check: The future markdown follows the exact heading sequence.
The future page is about real cougar sign, livestock conflict, fear, restraint, and table action.
The prompt contains no finished page prose.
All language belongs to grounded 1899 Jefferson State western play.
